seeds.citation: Santa-Clara, Pedro, and Rossen Valkanov, 2003, "The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market," Journal of Finance 58(5), 1841-1872.
seeds.conclusion_plain: Since 1927, US stocks have earned about 9 percentage points more per year over T-bills under Democratic presidents than Republican ones (16 points for the equal-weighted index, and up to 22 points for the smallest firms), and the gap builds steadily through each term rather than jumping at election dates. The authors show this is not explained by the business cycle, by election-day surprises, or by higher risk under Democrats - the market seems repeatedly and positively surprised by Democratic administrations, leaving the return gap an unexplained puzzle.
seeds.fit: This is the canonical RQ2 paper (returns by president type / the presidential puzzle), and its finding that returns are NOT concentrated around election dates also disciplines RQ1/RQ5 event-window interpretations.

refs.title: United States investment returns during Democratic and Republican administrations, 1928-1993
refs.authors_year: Hensel & Ziemba (1995), Financial Analysts Journal
refs.why: Direct RQ2 precursor: documents party-by-party return differences across asset classes, including the small-cap effect we see in the size-decile monotonicity.

refs.title: Common stock returns and presidential elections
refs.authors_year: Huang (1985), Financial Analysts Journal
refs.why: Early presidential-election-cycle evidence on stock returns - feeds RQ2 and the RQ5 presidential-cycle seasonality (which half of the term pays).

refs.title: Political-economic cycles in the U.S. stock market
refs.authors_year: Herbst & Slinkman (1984), Financial Analysts Journal
refs.why: Tests the four-year political-economic cycle in equities - the seasonality backbone of RQ5 (midterm/cycle patterns).

refs.title: Presidential politics, stocks, bonds, bills, and inflation
refs.authors_year: Chittenden, Jensen & Johnson (1999), Journal of Portfolio Management
refs.why: Extends the party-regime comparison beyond equities to bonds, bills, and inflation - useful for RQ2 Sharpe/vol-by-administration framing.

refs.title: Political Cycles and the Macroeconomy
refs.authors_year: Alesina, Roubini & Cohen (1997), MIT Press
refs.why: The rational-partisan-theory foundation: why Democratic vs Republican policy regimes should differ macro-economically - the theory layer under RQ2 and RQ6 partisan winners.

refs.title: The neglected effect of fiscal policy on stock and bond returns
refs.authors_year: Tavares & Valkanov (2001), UCLA Anderson working paper
refs.why: The authors' own conjectured mechanism (fiscal/regulatory policy moving returns) - the channel through which RQ6-style policy shocks reprice markets.

seeds.citation: Pastor, Lubos, and Pietro Veronesi. 2020. "Political Cycles and Stock Returns." Journal of Political Economy 128 (11): 4011-4045. (NBER Working Paper 23184.)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Stock returns and economic growth are higher under Democratic presidents not because Democrats manage the economy better, but because voters tend to elect Democrats when they are fearful (e.g., after crises), which is exactly when markets compensate investors with higher future returns; Republicans tend to win when investors feel safe and expected returns are low. Timing of who gets elected, not what presidents do, explains the "presidential puzzle" -- consistent with the fact that control of Congress shows no such return pattern.
seeds.fit: Core paper for RQ2 (president-type regimes / the presidential puzzle): it provides the leading risk-aversion-based explanation for why returns differ by party in power, and frames RQ5's presidential-cycle seasonality.

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003), Journal of Finance
refs.why: The original empirical documentation of higher excess equity returns under Democrats -- the anchor fact for RQ2.

refs.title: Presidents and the US Economy: An Econometric Exploration
refs.authors_year: Blinder & Watson (2016), American Economic Review
refs.why: Rigorous measurement of the Democrat-Republican gap in GDP growth and macro outcomes by administration -- the macro counterpart to RQ2's return/vol/Sharpe comparison.

refs.title: United States Investment Returns during Democratic and Republican Administrations, 1928-1993
refs.authors_year: Hensel & Ziemba (1995), Financial Analysts Journal
refs.why: Long-sample asset returns split by party in power (including small-cap effects) -- directly matches RQ2's 1928-onward president comparison.

refs.title: Are Policy Platforms Capitalized into Equity Prices? Evidence from the Bush/Gore 2000 Presidential Election
refs.authors_year: Knight (2006), Journal of Public Economics
refs.why: Shows party-favored sectors reprice as election odds move -- direct evidence for RQ6's sector/industry winners by winning party and election repricing.

refs.title: Partisan Impacts on the Economy: Evidence from Prediction Markets and Close Elections
refs.authors_year: Snowberg, Wolfers & Zitzewitz (2007), Quarterly Journal of Economics
refs.why: High-frequency market repricing around election-outcome surprises (prediction-market identification) -- serves RQ1's de-risk/re-risk around elections and RQ6 event-study design.

refs.title: Precarious Politics and Return Volatility
refs.authors_year: Boutchkova, Doshi, Durnev & Molchanov (2012), Review of Financial Studies
refs.why: Links elections and political risk to industry-level return volatility -- supports RQ2's volatility-by-regime analysis and RQ6's sector sensitivity to political shocks.

seeds.citation: Pastor, L., and Veronesi, P. (2013). Political Uncertainty and Risk Premia. Journal of Financial Economics, 110(3), 520-545. (NBER Working Paper 17464)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Investors demand extra return for holding stocks when it is unclear what the government will do next, and this premium is biggest when the economy is weak. Political uncertainty also makes stocks both jumpier and more likely to move together, because it erodes the safety net investors expect the government to provide in bad times.
seeds.fit: Theoretical backbone for RQ1 and RQ2: it explains why money de-risks ahead of elections (political uncertainty is priced) and frames returns/volatility differences across political regimes.

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003), Journal of Finance
refs.why: The canonical presidential-puzzle paper - higher excess returns under Democratic presidents - which is RQ2 verbatim.

refs.title: Government Spending, Political Cycles, and the Cross-Section of Stock Returns
refs.authors_year: Belo, Gala & Li (2013), Journal of Financial Economics
refs.why: Shows which industries win under each party (government-exposure tilt flips Democrat vs Republican) - direct input to RQ2 and RQ6 sector-winner analysis.

refs.title: Political Elections and the Resolution of Uncertainty: The International Evidence
refs.authors_year: Pantzalis, Stangeland & Turtle (2000), Journal of Banking and Finance
refs.why: Documents the pre-election run-up / uncertainty-resolution return pattern across countries - the de-risk-before, re-risk-after template for RQ1 and RQ5.

refs.title: Presidential Election Uncertainty and Common Stock Returns in the United States
refs.authors_year: Li & Born (2006), Journal of Financial Research
refs.why: US-specific evidence that election uncertainty raises returns and volatility ahead of presidential elections - core RQ1/RQ2 event-window evidence.

refs.title: Corporate Financial Policy under Political Uncertainty: International Evidence from National Elections
refs.authors_year: Julio & Yook (2012), Journal of Finance
refs.why: Firms cut investment ahead of national elections and resume after - the corporate-side mirror of the de-risk/re-risk flow pattern in RQ1.

refs.title: Precarious Politics and Return Volatility
refs.authors_year: Boutchkova, Doshi, Durnev & Molchanov (2012), Review of Financial Studies
refs.why: Politically sensitive industries (incl. trade-dependent ones) show higher volatility around political events - supports RQ6 trade-shock repricing and the sector lens in RQ2.

seeds.citation: Kelly, Bryan T., Lubos Pastor, and Pietro Veronesi, 2016, "The Price of Political Uncertainty: Theory and Evidence from the Option Market," Journal of Finance 71(5), 2417-2480 (NBER Working Paper 19812).
seeds.conclusion_plain: Options whose lifetimes span elections and global summits cost more than otherwise-similar options, meaning investors pay a real premium for insurance against political surprises. That insurance gets even more expensive when the economy is weak and the political outcome is more uncertain, confirming that political risk carries a price markets cannot diversify away.
seeds.fit: Anchors RQ1/RQ2: it shows investors hedge election windows in advance (the pricing counterpart of de-risk-before/re-risk-after flows) and supplies the Pastor-Veronesi theory behind election-period risk premia and the presidential puzzle.

refs.title: The presidential puzzle: Political cycles and the stock market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003), Journal of Finance
refs.why: The canonical RQ2 paper: documents higher excess equity returns under Democratic presidents, the exact 'presidential puzzle' our study targets.

refs.title: Policy uncertainty, irreversibility, and cross-border flows of capital
refs.authors_year: Julio & Yook (2013), working paper, London Business School
refs.why: Directly RQ1: shows cross-border capital flows drop around national elections and recover after uncertainty resolves.

refs.title: Government spending, political cycles, and the cross-section of stock returns
refs.authors_year: Belo, Gala & Li (2013), Journal of Financial Economics
refs.why: RQ2/RQ6: which sectors/firms win by ruling party - government-exposed firms earn different returns under Democratic vs Republican administrations.

refs.title: Stock market volatility around national elections
refs.authors_year: Bialkowski, Gottschalk & Wisniewski (2008), Journal of Banking and Finance
refs.why: RQ1/RQ2/RQ5: cross-country evidence that equity volatility spikes in election windows, the risk backdrop for de-risk/re-risk flow patterns.

refs.title: Political elections and the resolution of uncertainty: The international evidence
refs.authors_year: Pantzalis, Stangeland & Turtle (2000), Journal of Banking and Finance
refs.why: RQ1/RQ5: finds positive abnormal returns in the weeks before elections as uncertainty resolves, an early version of the re-risking pattern.

refs.title: Output, stock volatility, and political uncertainty in a natural experiment: Germany, 1880-1940
refs.authors_year: Bittlingmayer (1998), Journal of Finance
refs.why: RQ4: links political/regime turmoil spanning war buildup periods to stock volatility, historical evidence for the war-puzzle channel.

seeds.citation: Goodell, J.W., & Vähämaa, S. (2013). US presidential elections and implied volatility: The role of political uncertainty. Journal of Banking & Finance, 37(3), 1108-1117.
seeds.conclusion_plain: Using election prediction-market prices over five US presidential races, the paper finds that stock-market implied volatility (VIX) rises as the eventual winner's chances of victory rise, even after accounting for how uncertain the race itself is. In plain terms: the election process itself makes investors nervous, because they are repricing what the likely next president will mean for economic policy, and that anxiety fades once the outcome is settled.
seeds.fit: Primarily RQ2 (election-driven volatility regimes around presidential races) with direct support for RQ1's mechanism — political uncertainty is what drives pre-election de-risking and post-election re-risking.

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003)
refs.why: The canonical RQ2 paper: documents higher excess equity returns under Democratic than Republican presidents, the exact 'presidential puzzle' our RQ2 targets.

refs.title: Resolving the Presidential Puzzle
refs.authors_year: Sy & Al Zaman (2011)
refs.why: Directly extends RQ2: argues the Democrat-Republican return gap is compensation for differences in risk, testing whether the puzzle survives risk adjustment.

refs.title: Are Policy Platforms Capitalized into Equity Prices? Evidence from the Bush/Gore 2000 Presidential Election
refs.authors_year: Knight (2006)
refs.why: RQ6: shows party platforms reprice specific stocks/sectors as the winner's probability moves — the template for sector winners by winning party.

refs.title: Political Elections and the Resolution of Uncertainty: The International Evidence
refs.authors_year: Pantzalis, Stangeland & Turtle (2000)
refs.why: RQ1/RQ5: documents the cross-country pattern of equity behavior into and out of elections as uncertainty resolves — the de-risk-before/re-risk-after shape.

refs.title: Political Uncertainty and Corporate Investment Cycles
refs.authors_year: Julio & Yook (2012)
refs.why: RQ1: shows firms cut investment ahead of elections and resume after, the real-economy counterpart of pre-election de-risking in fund flows.

refs.title: US Elections and Monthly Stock Market Returns
refs.authors_year: Jones & Banning (2009)
refs.why: RQ5: examines monthly US stock returns around elections, bearing directly on midterm/presidential-cycle seasonality claims.

seeds.citation: Snowberg, Erik, Justin Wolfers, and Eric Zitzewitz (2007), "Partisan Impacts on the Economy: Evidence from Prediction Markets and Close Elections," Quarterly Journal of Economics 122(2): 807-829 (NBER Working Paper No. 12073).
seeds.conclusion_plain: Using the surprise swings in election-night odds in 2000 and 2004 as natural experiments (plus prediction-market data on every presidential election since 1880), the paper finds that markets genuinely expect policy to differ by party: a Republican win was priced as roughly 2-3% higher stock values, along with higher bond yields and oil prices. This shows investors do not believe the two parties converge to the same policies, and it cuts against the long-horizon finding that realized stock returns are higher under Democrats.
seeds.fit: Core RQ2 evidence (president-type effects on equities/yields; the causal counterpoint to the presidential puzzle), with spillover to RQ6 (markets pricing party-specific policy).

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003), Journal of Finance
refs.why: The canonical presidential-puzzle paper (higher excess returns under Democrats) that RQ2 is built around; Snowberg et al. directly contest it.

refs.title: Are Policy Platforms Capitalized into Equity Prices? Evidence from the Bush/Gore 2000 Presidential Election
refs.authors_year: Knight (2006/2007), Journal of Public Economics
refs.why: Identifies which specific stocks win under each party's platform — direct evidence for RQ6 sector/industry winners by winning party.

refs.title: The Jeffords Effect
refs.authors_year: Jayachandran (2006), Journal of Law and Economics
refs.why: Shows a sudden partisan shift in Senate control repriced politically exposed firms — firm-level partisan return evidence for RQ2/RQ6.

refs.title: Using Markets to Inform Policy: The Case of the Iraq War
refs.authors_year: Wolfers & Zitzewitz (2005), mimeo, University of Pennsylvania
refs.why: Uses war-probability markets to measure how Iraq war odds moved oil and equity prices — directly serves RQ4 (war buildup vs outbreak pricing).

refs.title: Political Cycles and the Macroeconomy
refs.authors_year: Alesina, Roubini & Cohen (1997), MIT Press
refs.why: The foundational partisan political-business-cycle framework underlying RQ2 regime comparisons and RQ5 electoral-cycle seasonality.

refs.title: Estimating the Economic Impact of Political Party Competition in the 1992 British Election
refs.authors_year: Herron (2000), American Journal of Political Science
refs.why: Election-probability-to-market-repricing design in a non-US setting — a cross-country analogue for RQ2 and election repricing in RQ1.

seeds.citation: Wagner, A.F., Zeckhauser, R.J., and Ziegler, A. (2018). Company Stock Price Reactions to the 2016 Election Shock: Trump, Taxes, and Trade. Journal of Financial Economics 130(2), 428-451. (NBER Working Paper 23152, Feb 2017; full PDF read at https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w23152/w23152.pdf)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Trump's surprise 2016 win instantly repriced individual stocks along expected policy lines: companies with high tax burdens, high market sensitivity, and domestic focus gained, while healthcare, pharma, and import-dependent or internationally oriented firms lost. Investors priced in the growth and tax implications within a day, but needed weeks to work out what the trade-policy shift meant for specific companies.
seeds.fit: Core anchor for RQ6 (an election as a tariff/tax repricing event with clear sector winners by winning party), with supporting context for RQ1/RQ2 on post-election re-risking.

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003), Journal of Finance
refs.why: The defining paper for RQ2's presidential puzzle: excess equity returns are higher under Democratic presidents.

refs.title: Presidential Elections and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Niederhoffer, Gibbs & Bullock (1970), Financial Analysts Journal
refs.why: Earliest systematic evidence on market behavior around presidential elections, feeding RQ2 and RQ5 cycle/seasonality patterns.

refs.title: What Do Financial Markets Think of the 2016 Election?
refs.authors_year: Wolfers & Zitzewitz (2016), working paper
refs.why: Links election-probability shifts (prediction markets, debates, election night) to real-time asset repricing, serving RQ1/RQ2 on de-risk/re-risk around elections.

refs.title: Using Financial Data to Measure Effects of Regulation
refs.authors_year: Schwert (1981), Journal of Law and Economics
refs.why: Methodological foundation for treating policy/regulatory announcements as stock-price repricing events, the event-study backbone of RQ6 tariff shocks.

refs.title: Watch What I Do, Not What I Say: The Unintended Consequences of the Homeland Investment Act
refs.authors_year: Dharmapala, Foley & Forbes (2011), Journal of Finance
refs.why: Shows how a one-off tax/repatriation policy shock moved corporate capital flows, useful comparison case for RQ6 policy-shock winners.

seeds.citation: Chan, Kam Fong, and Terry Marsh (2021), "Asset prices, midterm elections, and political uncertainty," Journal of Financial Economics 141(1), 276-296. DOI: 10.1016/j.jfineco.2021.03.007
seeds.conclusion_plain: In the months right after U.S. midterm elections, stock returns, mutual-fund inflows, and real investment are all significantly higher while Treasury bond premiums fall, because the election resolves a large dose of political uncertainty. Midterms turn out to matter more for asset prices than presidential elections, and the usual risk-return (beta) relationship only shows up in those post-midterm months.
seeds.fit: Core paper for RQ5 (post-midterm equity pattern / presidential-cycle seasonality), with direct spillover to RQ1 via its post-midterm mutual-fund-flow evidence.

refs.title: Presidents and the US economy: an econometric exploration (American Economic Review)
refs.authors_year: Blinder & Watson, 2016
refs.why: Canonical evidence that the economy performs better under Democratic presidents — the macro side of RQ2's presidential puzzle.

refs.title: Washington meets Wall Street: a closer examination of the presidential cycle puzzle (Journal of International Money and Finance)
refs.authors_year: Kräussl, Lucas, Rijsbergen, van der Sluis & Vrugt, 2014
refs.why: Directly re-examines the presidential-cycle return puzzle — the exact anomaly behind RQ2 and RQ5.

refs.title: Government spending, political cycles, and the cross section of stock returns (Journal of Financial Economics)
refs.authors_year: Belo, Gala & Li, 2013
refs.why: Shows government-exposed industries earn different returns under Democratic vs Republican administrations — partisan sector winners for RQ6/RQ2.

refs.title: Policy uncertainty, irreversibility, and cross-border flows of capital (Journal of International Economics)
refs.authors_year: Julio & Yook, 2016
refs.why: Documents FDI/capital flows dropping into elections and recovering after — the elections-and-capital-flows mechanism for RQ1.

refs.title: The price of political uncertainty: theory and evidence from the option market (Journal of Finance)
refs.authors_year: Kelly, Pástor & Veronesi, 2016
refs.why: Shows options embed a measurable premium for election-date political uncertainty — pricing backbone for RQ5/RQ1 event windows.

refs.title: News implied volatility and disaster concerns (Journal of Financial Economics)
refs.authors_year: Manela & Moreira, 2017
refs.why: Builds a news-based (NVIX) measure where war concerns drive priced disaster risk — links war/geopolitics to market premia for RQ4/RQ3.

seeds.citation: Anderson, W., Bialkowski, J., & Wagner, M. (2023). Midterm elections and stock returns. Finance Research Letters, 55, 103825. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.frl.2023.103825 (published version: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1544612323001988; open working paper: https://econpapers.repec.org/paper/cbteconwp/23_2f05.htm)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Across 49 US industry portfolios from 1926-2022, stocks do earn noticeably higher returns around midterm elections, but the extra return disappears once you adjust for ordinary market risk - it is simply payment for bearing election-time risk that hits the whole market. So there is no free lunch: neither rotating into particular sectors nor timing the midterm election produces higher risk-adjusted returns.
seeds.fit: Directly serves RQ5 (post-midterm equity pattern / presidential-cycle seasonality) as the key skeptical, risk-adjusted counterpoint, and informs RQ6's sector-rotation question by showing industry-level midterm gains are not exploitable.

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003), Journal of Finance
refs.why: The canonical presidential-puzzle paper (higher excess returns under Democrats) - the core citation for RQ2 president-type regimes.

refs.title: Asset Prices, Midterm Elections, and Political Uncertainty
refs.authors_year: Chan & Marsh (2021), Journal of Financial Economics
refs.why: Shows midterm elections resolve political uncertainty and drive the post-midterm equity rally - the main mechanism paper for RQ5.

refs.title: Policy Uncertainty, Irreversibility, and Cross-Border Flows of Capital
refs.authors_year: Julio & Yook (2016), Journal of International Economics
refs.why: Links election-driven policy uncertainty to reduced cross-border capital flows - direct evidence for RQ1 de-risk-before/re-risk-after flow behavior.

refs.title: Political Cycles in US Industry Returns
refs.authors_year: Stangl & Jacobsen (2007), Journal of International Finance and Economics
refs.why: Tests which industries win under each party and across the presidential cycle, arguing apparent political cycles are time-varying risk - feeds RQ6 (sector winners by party) and RQ2.

refs.title: Government Spending, Political Cycles and the Cross Section of Stock Returns
refs.authors_year: Belo, Gala & Li (2013), Journal of Financial Economics
refs.why: Government-exposure industries earn higher returns under Democrats and lower under Republicans - a partisan sector-return result central to RQ6.

refs.title: Presidents and the US Economy: An Econometric Exploration
refs.authors_year: Blinder & Watson (2016), American Economic Review
refs.why: Documents the Democrat-Republican GDP growth gap and tests explanations - the macro foundation for RQ2's presidential-regime comparisons.

seeds.citation: Kräussl, R., Lucas, A., Rijsbergen, D.R., van der Sluis, P.J., & Vrugt, E.B. (2014). Washington meets Wall Street: A closer examination of the presidential cycle puzzle. Journal of International Money and Finance, 43, 50-69.
seeds.conclusion_plain: U.S. stocks earn almost 10 percentage points more per year in the last two years of a presidential term than in the first two, and the authors cannot explain this with economic conditions, risk, investor mood, or evidence that presidents pump up the economy before elections. The pattern survives every test they throw at it and remains an unexplained puzzle.
seeds.fit: Core paper for RQ2 (presidential puzzle) and RQ5 (presidential-cycle seasonality: strong years 3-4 begin right after midterms); also tests the policy-manipulation mechanism behind both.

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market (Journal of Finance)
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003)
refs.why: The canonical RQ2 paper: documents higher excess returns under Democratic presidents, the original presidential puzzle.

refs.authors_year: Booth & Booth (2003)
refs.why: Tests whether the presidential-cycle return pattern is just business-cycle conditions — directly serves RQ2/RQ5 robustness.

refs.authors_year: Gärtner & Wellershoff (1995)
refs.why: Early documentation of the systematic election-cycle pattern in U.S. stock returns (low years 1-2, high years 3-4) for RQ5.

refs.authors_year: Campbell & Li (2004)
refs.why: Re-estimates the Democrat-vs-Republican return premium and questions its statistical reliability — key counterpoint for RQ2.

seeds.citation: Arakelyan, Maria, and Tatiana Evdokimova. 2025. "Elections Matter: Capital Flows and Political Cycles." IMF Working Paper No. 2025/243, International Monetary Fund, Washington, DC. DOI: 10.5089/9798229031813.001
seeds.conclusion_plain: Across 38 emerging markets (1990-2020), foreign private money coming into a country drops noticeably in the quarter around an election — investors pull back when they don't know who will win or what policies will follow. The drop is bigger and lasts longer when the incumbent loses or the election is chaotic (snap elections, violence), and it largely disappears in politically stable countries with strong institutions.
seeds.fit: Directly serves RQ1: academic anchor for "investors de-risk around elections" in capital-flow data — the cross-country generalization of our US election fund-flow de-risk/re-risk pattern.

refs.authors_year: Honig, Adam (2020), Journal of Money, Credit and Banking 52(2-3): 471-503
refs.why: The closest predecessor on elections driving capital-flow retrenchment — core RQ1 citation.

refs.authors_year: Julio, Brandon, and Youngsuk Yook (2016), Journal of International Economics 103: 13-26
refs.why: Shows cross-border capital (FDI) falls before elections and recovers after — the de-risk-before/re-risk-after mechanism for RQ1.

refs.authors_year: Frot, Emmanuel, and Javier Santiso (2013), Review of International Political Economy 20(1): 26-51
refs.why: Fund-manager-level evidence of portfolio reallocation around elections — nearest analogue to our country-fund rotation question in RQ1.

refs.title: Political Uncertainty and Corporate Investment Cycles
refs.authors_year: Julio, Brandon, and Youngsuk Yook (2012), The Journal of Finance 67(1): 45-83
refs.why: Canonical election-cycle paper: firms cut investment ahead of elections worldwide — underpins the pre-election de-risking logic in RQ1/RQ2.

refs.title: Stock Market Volatility around National Elections
refs.authors_year: Bialkowski, Jedrzej, Katrin Gottschalk, and Tomasz Piotr Wisniewski (2008), Journal of Banking and Finance 32(9): 1941-1953
refs.why: Documents the election-window volatility spike in equity markets — feeds RQ2 (volatility by political regime/event).

refs.title: Precarious Politics and Return Volatility
refs.authors_year: Boutchkova, Maria, Hitesh Doshi, Artyom Durnev, and Alexander Molchanov (2011), Review of Financial Studies 25(4): 1111-1154
refs.why: Links political risk to industry-level return volatility — bridges RQ2 (regime volatility) and RQ6 (which sectors reprice on politics).

seeds.citation: Koepke, R. (2019). What Drives Capital Flows to Emerging Markets? A Survey of the Empirical Literature. Journal of Economic Surveys, 33(2), 516-540. (Working-paper version read: MPRA Paper No. 75887, 2015; also SSRN abstract 2569249.)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Money moves into and out of emerging markets for two kinds of reasons: global conditions (mainly how scared investors are worldwide and rich-country interest rates) and conditions inside each country. Global fear and rate shifts dominate for stock and bond flows -- so foreign portfolio money exits fastest when global risk spikes -- while longer-term money like direct investment mostly follows a country's own growth.
seeds.fit: RQ1 -- gives the push/pull framework and evidence that risk-aversion shocks (the mechanism behind pre-election de-risking and post-election re-risking and country rotation) drive portfolio fund flows.

refs.authors_year: Papaioannou (2009), Journal of Development Economics
refs.why: The only reference directly tying politics and institutional quality to cross-border capital flows -- closest bridge to elections-as-flow-drivers (RQ1).

refs.authors_year: Fratzscher (2012), Journal of International Economics
refs.why: Shows risk shocks flip flows from risk-on to risk-off across countries -- the template for measuring de-risk/re-risk around election events (RQ1).

refs.authors_year: Forbes and Warnock (2012), Journal of International Economics
refs.why: Defines the episode taxonomy (surge/stop/flight/retrenchment) useful for classifying pre/post-election flow episodes (RQ1).

refs.why: Fund-level evidence on how mutual funds reallocate across countries under shocks -- direct method for country-level rotation like China-fund outflows (RQ1).

refs.authors_year: Rey (2013), Jackson Hole Symposium
refs.why: Establishes one global risk-appetite cycle driving flows everywhere -- needed control when isolating election-specific flow effects (RQ1).

refs.title: The Great Retrenchment: International Capital Flows during the Global Financial Crisis
refs.authors_year: Milesi-Ferretti and Tille (2011), Economic Policy
refs.why: Benchmark study of investors pulling money home in a risk shock -- comparator for sizing election-driven de-risking episodes (RQ1).

seeds.citation: Fratzscher, Marcel (2012). "Capital flows, push versus pull factors and the global financial crisis." Journal of International Economics, 88(2), 341-356. (Also NBER WP 17357 and ECB WP 1364.)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Using weekly fund-level portfolio flows to 50 countries, the paper finds that during the 2008 crisis global shocks (risk spikes, liquidity squeezes, key crisis events) pulled money out of emerging markets and into safe advanced economies, while in the 2009-10 recovery it was each country's own fundamentals, institutions and policies that determined where money flowed back. Capital-account openness mattered little, so good domestic policy, not capital controls, is what shields a country from global flow shocks.
seeds.fit: RQ1 — the methodological backbone: high-frequency (EPFR-style) fund flows reacting to discrete risk events with flight-to-safety and country-level rotation is exactly the template for measuring de-risk-before/re-risk-after and China-fund outflows around US elections.

refs.title: When in Peril, Retrench: Testing the Portfolio Channel of Contagion
refs.authors_year: Broner, Gelos & Reinhart (2006), Journal of International Economics
refs.why: Shows mutual funds systematically retrench from overweight countries when hit by shocks — the mechanism behind RQ1's de-risk-before-elections and country-rotation pattern.

refs.title: The Great Retrenchment: International Capital Flows during the Global Financial Crisis
refs.authors_year: Milesi-Ferretti & Tille (2010)
refs.why: Documents the country-level collapse and rebound of cross-border flows around a risk shock — the baseline de-risk/re-risk dynamic RQ1 tests around election events.

refs.title: Inflows of Capital to Developing Countries in the 1990s
refs.authors_year: Calvo, Leiderman & Reinhart (1996), Journal of Economic Perspectives
refs.why: The canonical push-vs-pull framework for deciding whether election-driven flows (e.g. China-fund outflows after a Trump win) are a US 'push' shock or country 'pull' repricing.

refs.title: Equity and bond flows to Latin America and Asia: the role of global and country factors
refs.authors_year: Chuhan, Claessens & Mamingi (1998), Journal of Development Economics
refs.why: Separates global vs country-specific drivers of equity and bond flows by destination — the empirical design for RQ1's country-level rotation analysis.

refs.title: Capital Flow Bonanzas: An Encompassing View of the Past and Present
refs.authors_year: Reinhart & Reinhart (2009), NBER International Seminar on Macroeconomics
refs.why: Characterizes surge/reversal cycles in capital flows — the re-risking surge after election uncertainty resolves is one such bonanza episode for RQ1.

refs.title: Asset Fire Sales and Purchases and the International Transmission of Financial Shocks
refs.authors_year: Jotikasthira, Lundblad & Ramadorai (2010, CEPR DP 7595; later JF 2012)
refs.why: Links investor redemptions from funds to forced cross-border trading — the fund-flow transmission channel RQ1 needs to connect election-driven redemptions to country returns.

seeds.citation: Forbes, Kristin J., and Francis E. Warnock (2012), "Capital Flow Waves: Surges, Stops, Flight, and Retrenchment," Journal of International Economics, 88(2), 235-251.
seeds.conclusion_plain: Big swings in cross-border investment money are driven mainly by global conditions — above all how risky the world feels — rather than by a country's own economic health: when global fear spikes, foreign investors stop sending money in and local investors pull their money back home. Capital controls do little to stop these waves.
seeds.fit: RQ1: provides the canonical taxonomy (surge/stop/flight/retrenchment) and the global-risk-driver evidence for framing de-risk-before / re-risk-after election fund flows and country-level rotation.

refs.title: When in peril, retrench: Testing the portfolio channel of contagion (Journal of International Economics 69(1), 203-230)
refs.authors_year: Broner, Gelos & Reinhart (2006)
refs.why: Shows fund managers pull back toward benchmark/home positions in risky times — the exact de-risk/retrench mechanism RQ1 posits around elections.

refs.title: Are Daily Cross-Border Equity Flows Pushed or Pulled? (Review of Economics and Statistics 86(3), 641-657)
refs.authors_year: Griffin, Nardari & Stulz (2004)
refs.why: High-frequency equity-flow push/pull decomposition — the method needed to attribute flow moves around election dates to local vs global drivers (RQ1).

refs.title: Mutual Fund Investment in Emerging Markets: An Overview (World Bank Economic Review 15(2), 315-340)
refs.authors_year: Kaminsky, Lyons & Schmukler (2001)
refs.why: Documents country-level mutual-fund flow behavior in emerging markets — direct template for the China-fund outflow rotation in RQ1.

refs.title: The new wave of private capital inflows: Push or pull? (Journal of Development Economics 48(2), 389-418)
refs.authors_year: Fernandez-Arias (1996)
refs.why: Classic push-vs-pull framework for whether country flows respond to domestic events (e.g., political outcomes) or global conditions (RQ1).

refs.title: The great retrenchment: international capital flows during the global financial crisis (Economic Policy 26(66), 289-346)
refs.authors_year: Milesi-Ferretti & Tille (2011)
refs.why: Anatomy of a synchronized global de-risking and subsequent re-risking in flows — the dynamic RQ1 tests around US election dates.

refs.title: Risk, uncertainty and monetary policy (Journal of Monetary Economics 60(7), 771-788)
refs.authors_year: Bekaert, Hoerova & Lo Duca (2013)
refs.why: Splits the VIX into risk aversion vs uncertainty — useful for separating election-uncertainty effects on flows and safe-haven (gold) demand (RQ1, RQ3).

seeds.citation: Chari, Anusha, Karlye Dilts Stedman, and Christian Lundblad. "Global Fund Flows and Emerging Market Tail Risk." NBER Working Paper No. 30577, October 2022. (Note: the seed's w31143 number is a different paper — Chari's solo survey "Global Risk, Non-Bank Financial Intermediation, and Emerging Market Vulnerabilities"; the correct number for this title is w30577. Landing page: https://www.nber.org/papers/w30577; also SSRN 4245851.)
seeds.conclusion_plain: When global investors are hit by shocks to risk or risk appetite, mutual funds and ETFs pull money out of emerging markets in ways that hit the worst-case (tail) outcomes much harder than the average: rising risk aversion triggers sudden stops while macro-uncertainty shocks trigger capital flight. Passive funds and ETFs, which must mechanically track benchmarks, react far more than active funds, so much of emerging-market tail risk is driven by forced, non-discretionary rebalancing rather than deliberate investor decisions.
seeds.fit: RQ1 — gives the mechanism and measurement framework (EPFR-style fund flows, flows-at-risk, sudden stop vs flight taxonomy) for how global funds de-risk/re-risk and rotate across countries when risk events like elections hit.

refs.title: Capital flow waves: Surges, stops, flight, and retrenchment
refs.authors_year: Forbes & Warnock (2012), Journal of International Economics 88(2)
refs.why: Canonical taxonomy of extreme capital-flow episodes — the standard framework for classifying de-risk/re-risk flow moves around events like elections (RQ1).

refs.title: Capital flows, push versus pull factors and the global financial crisis
refs.authors_year: Fratzscher (2012), Journal of International Economics 88(2)
refs.why: Uses fund-level flow data to separate global push from country-specific pull factors — directly applicable to country-level rotation such as China-fund outflows after a US election (RQ1).

refs.title: Capital flows at risk: Taming the ebbs and flows
refs.authors_year: Gelos, Gornicka, Koepke, Sahay & Sgherri (2019), IMF Working Paper
refs.why: The flows-at-risk framework for quantifying tail outflows — a ready-made method for measuring election-window flow tail risk (RQ1).

refs.title: International asset allocations and capital flows: The benchmark effect
refs.authors_year: Raddatz, Schmukler & Williams (2017), Journal of International Economics 108
refs.why: Shows benchmark-driven funds mechanically reallocate across countries — explains how country weights amplify politically triggered rotation out of specific markets (RQ1).

refs.title: How ETFs amplify the global financial cycle in emerging markets
refs.authors_year: Converse, Levy Yeyati & Williams (2020), International Finance Discussion Papers 1268
refs.why: ETF flows transmit global shocks into country-level EM flows more strongly than mutual funds — the amplification channel for post-election de-risk/re-risk waves (RQ1).

refs.title: Asset fire sales and purchases and the international transmission of funding shocks
refs.authors_year: Jotikasthira, Lundblad & Ramadorai (2012), Journal of Finance 67(6)
refs.why: Fund redemptions force fire sales that move prices in unrelated EM countries — the mechanism by which a US political shock reprices foreign markets via fund flows (RQ1).

seeds.citation: Meeuwis, Maarten, Jonathan A. Parker, Antoinette Schoar, and Duncan Simester, 2022, Belief Disagreement and Portfolio Choice, Journal of Finance 77(6), 3191-3247. NBER Working Paper 25108.
seeds.conclusion_plain: After Trump's surprise 2016 win, Republican-leaning investors moved their retirement portfolios into stocks while Democrat-leaning investors moved into safe assets — the same news produced opposite trades because the two groups read it through different views of the world, not because their incomes or taxes were differently exposed. The average shift was small and driven by a minority of attentive, self-directed investors, and the unsurprising 2012 election produced no comparable partisan rebalancing.
seeds.fit: Core evidence for RQ1: households de-risk/re-risk around elections along partisan lines (micro-level mechanism behind election-driven fund flows), with side relevance to RQ2 regime narratives.

refs.title: Unequal rewards to firms: Stock market responses to the Trump election and the 2017 corporate tax reform
refs.authors_year: Wagner, Zeckhauser & Ziegler (2018), AEA Papers and Proceedings
refs.why: Directly measures which firms/sectors repriced as winners and losers after the Trump election and tax reform — RQ6 (sector winners by winning party) and RQ1 election repricing.

refs.title: Inequality aversion, populism, and the backlash against globalization
refs.authors_year: Pastor & Veronesi (2021, Journal of Finance)
refs.why: Pastor-Veronesi political asset-pricing framework linking political regimes and globalization backlash to asset prices — underpins RQ2 (presidential puzzle) and RQ6 (trade-policy repricing).

refs.title: Red and blue investing: Values and finance
refs.authors_year: Hong & Kostovetsky (2012), Journal of Financial Economics
refs.why: Shows fund managers' political affiliation tilts their sector holdings — partisan sector flows/returns evidence for RQ1 and RQ6.

refs.title: Political climate, optimism, and investment decisions
refs.authors_year: Bonaparte, Kumar & Page (2017), Journal of Financial Markets
refs.why: Investors get more optimistic and take more portfolio risk when their party holds power — the de-risk/re-risk-by-administration mechanism for RQ1 and RQ2.

refs.title: The globalization risk premium
refs.authors_year: Barrot, Loualiche & Sauvagnat (2019), Journal of Finance
refs.why: Prices trade/import-competition exposure in equities — the baseline for treating tariff and trade-policy shocks as repricing events (RQ6).

refs.title: Stock market aversion? Political preferences and stock market participation
refs.authors_year: Kaustia & Torstila (2011), Journal of Financial Economics
refs.why: Political preferences causally shift equity participation — supports the partisan-flows channel around political turnover for RQ1/RQ2.

seeds.citation: Addoum, Jawad M., and Alok Kumar. 2016. "Political Sentiment and Predictable Returns." Review of Financial Studies 29(12): 3471-3518.
seeds.conclusion_plain: When the party in the White House changes, investors systematically shift their portfolios toward industries they believe the new party favors, and these demand shifts make returns in politically sensitive industries predictable. A strategy exploiting this earned roughly 6% a year risk-adjusted over 1939-2011, with the effect about twice as strong when the challenger party wins (especially Democrat-to-Republican transitions) and concentrated around election months and years 1 and 4 of the presidential term.
seeds.fit: Core for RQ2 (party-in-power return regimes) and RQ6 (industry winners by winning party); the investor-demand-shift mechanism also underpins RQ1's rotation-around-elections story.

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara and Valkanov, 2003 (Journal of Finance)
refs.why: The canonical presidential-puzzle paper (higher excess returns under Democrats) — directly the anchor for RQ2 and the presidential-cycle seasonality in RQ5.

refs.title: Government Spending, Political Cycles, and the Cross Section of Stock Returns
refs.authors_year: Belo, Gala, and Li, 2013 (Journal of Financial Economics)
refs.why: Shows firms with high government-spending exposure earn different returns under Democratic vs Republican presidencies — partisan sector winners for RQ2/RQ6.

refs.title: Political Uncertainty and Risk Premia
refs.authors_year: Pastor and Veronesi, 2013 (Journal of Financial Economics)
refs.why: Theory of how political/policy uncertainty commands a risk premium — the framework behind de-risking before elections (RQ1) and tariff/policy repricing (RQ6).

refs.title: The Price of Political Uncertainty: Theory and Evidence from the Option Market
refs.authors_year: Kelly, Pastor, and Veronesi, 2015 working paper (pub. 2016, Journal of Finance)
refs.why: Options written across election dates carry measurable political-uncertainty premia — direct evidence for pre-election hedging/de-risking in RQ1.

refs.title: Red and Blue Investing: Values and Finance
refs.authors_year: Hong and Kostovetsky, 2012 (Journal of Financial Economics)
refs.why: Democratic-leaning fund managers tilt portfolios away from socially sensitive industries — partisan portfolio rotation by professional money, feeding RQ1 and RQ6.

refs.title: Corporate Political Contributions and Stock Returns
refs.authors_year: Cooper, Gulen, and Ovtchinnikov, 2010 (Journal of Finance)
refs.why: Firms connected to the winning party via contributions earn abnormal returns — evidence on who wins by winning party for RQ6.

seeds.citation: Hong, H., Kostovetsky, L., 2012. Red and blue investing: Values and finance. Journal of Financial Economics 103(1), 1-19. (Working-paper PDF loaded from Hong's Columbia page; SSRN abstract 1214382; ScienceDirect DOI 10.1016/j.jfineco.2011.01.006)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Mutual fund (and hedge fund) managers who donate to Democrats hold noticeably less of their portfolios in "socially irresponsible" stocks - tobacco, guns and defense, natural resources - than Republican-donor or non-donor managers, even in funds that are not explicitly socially responsible. Since these are large professional managers who help set prices, the authors conclude that politics-driven "closet SRI" is widespread enough that values-based shunning can have a real impact on stock prices.
seeds.fit: RQ2 (partisan regimes in money management - political identity directly shapes professional portfolios), with spillover to RQ4/RQ6 via Democratic managers' systematic underweighting of defense and other politically coded sectors.

refs.title: The price of sin: The effects of social norms on markets
refs.authors_year: Hong, H., Kacperczyk, M. (2009, Journal of Financial Economics; cited as forthcoming)
refs.why: Shows shunned 'sin' stocks - including guns and defense - earn higher returns when norm-constrained institutions avoid them; direct input to RQ4 defense-sector returns and RQ6 sector winners by political coding.

refs.title: Patriotism in your portfolio?
refs.authors_year: Morse, A., Shive, S. (cited as 2006 working paper; published 2011, Journal of Financial Markets)
refs.why: Patriotism/political sentiment drives home bias in equity allocation - a politics-driven country-level allocation channel for RQ1's country rotation around elections.

refs.title: Investing in socially responsible mutual funds
refs.authors_year: Geczy, C., Stambaugh, R., Levin, D. (2007 working paper, Wharton)
refs.why: Quantifies the performance cost of constraining portfolios on values - the benchmark for whether politically tilted portfolios sacrifice Sharpe (RQ2).

refs.title: Melting pot or salad bowl: Some evidence from U.S. investments abroad
refs.authors_year: Bhattacharya, U., Groznik, P. (2008, Journal of Financial Markets)
refs.why: Identity ties (not fundamentals) drive US cross-border investment allocation by destination country - mechanism evidence for RQ1's country-level fund rotation (e.g. China-fund flows).

refs.title: Investor diversification and international equity markets
refs.authors_year: French, K., Poterba, J. (1991, American Economic Review P&P)
refs.why: The canonical home-bias baseline for country-level equity allocation - the null against which RQ1's election-driven country rotation should be measured.

seeds.citation: Della Corte, Pasquale, and Hsuan Fu. "Presidential Cycles and Exchange Rates." Working paper, Imperial College London & Universite Laval, December 2020 draft (also SSRN abstract 4769498, March 2024 draft).
seeds.conclusion_plain: Over ~40 years and 25 currencies, the US dollar on average strengthened about 4.3% a year under Democratic presidents and weakened about 1.3% a year under Republicans — a gap of roughly 5.6% per year that is not explained by interest rates, inflation, or the state of the economy. The authors attribute it to trade policy: higher tariffs and trade-policy uncertainty under Republicans get priced into currencies as extra risk.
seeds.fit: Serves RQ2 (president-type return regimes / the presidential puzzle, extended from stocks to FX) and feeds RQ6 via its tariff and trade-policy-uncertainty channel.

refs.title: The Presidential Puzzle: Political Cycles and the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003), Journal of Finance
refs.why: The original presidential-puzzle paper (higher excess equity returns under Democrats) — the core citation for RQ2.

refs.title: Political Cycles and Stock Returns
refs.authors_year: Pastor & Veronesi (2020), Journal of Political Economy
refs.why: Leading explanation of the presidential puzzle via time-varying risk aversion and election outcomes — direct RQ2 material.

refs.title: Presidents and the US Economy: An Econometric Exploration
refs.authors_year: Blinder & Watson (2016), American Economic Review
refs.why: Documents the Democrat-Republican gap in real economic performance and tests explanations — the macro backbone for RQ2 regime comparisons.

refs.title: Global Political Uncertainty and Asset Prices
refs.authors_year: Brogaard, Dai, Ngo & Zhang (2019), Review of Financial Studies
refs.why: Shows US election uncertainty spills into global asset prices and risk premia — supports RQ1's cross-country de-risk/re-risk rotation around elections.

refs.title: The Return to Protectionism
refs.authors_year: Fajgelbaum, Goldberg, Kennedy & Khandelwal (2019), Quarterly Journal of Economics
refs.why: Benchmark estimate of the 2018-19 tariff shock's price and welfare effects — anchors RQ6 on tariff shocks as repricing events.

refs.title: The Economic Effects of Trade Policy Uncertainty
refs.authors_year: Caldara, Iacoviello, Molligo, Prestipino & Raffo (2020), Journal of Monetary Economics
refs.why: Quantifies how trade-policy uncertainty depresses investment and activity — the uncertainty channel behind RQ6 tariff repricing.

seeds.citation: Aizenman, Joshua, and Jamel Saadaoui. "How Institutions Interact with Exchange Rates After the 2024 US Presidential Election: New High-Frequency Evidence." NBER Working Paper No. 33193, November 2024 (revised November 2025). DOI: 10.3386/w33193. PDF: https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w33193/w33193.pdf
seeds.conclusion_plain: After Trump's 2024 win, nearly every one of 73 currencies fell against the dollar, and - counterintuitively - countries with stronger, more democratic institutions saw bigger and longer-lasting drops. The authors read this as markets repricing the world order: with the US stepping back as guarantor of rules-based institutions, good institutions stopped acting as a safety buffer and instead became a source of exposure.
seeds.fit: Serves RQ1 directly (cross-country money/currency repricing and rotation immediately around a US election outcome), and supports RQ6's framing of an election as a discrete trade-policy repricing event.

refs.title: Real exchange rate cycles around elections
refs.authors_year: Stein, Streb, and Ghezzi (2005), Economics & Politics 17(3)
refs.why: Documents systematic currency cycles around election dates - the FX analogue of RQ1's de-risk-before / re-risk-after pattern.

refs.title: Elections and the Timing of Devaluations
refs.authors_year: Stein and Streb (2004), Journal of International Economics 63(1)
refs.why: Shows governments delay devaluations until after elections - direct evidence that money and policy reprice around election dates (RQ1).

refs.title: Elections and exchange rate policy cycles
refs.authors_year: Bonomo and Terra (2005), Economics & Politics 17(2)
refs.why: Models electorally driven exchange-rate policy cycles, feeding RQ1's election-window positioning story and RQ5's cycle-seasonality framing.

refs.title: The political business cycle
refs.authors_year: Nordhaus (1975), Review of Economic Studies 42(2)
refs.why: Foundational political-cycle paper underpinning RQ2 (administration regimes / presidential puzzle) and RQ5 (presidential-cycle seasonality).

refs.title: Do exchange rates influence voting? Evidence from elections and survey experiments in democracies
refs.authors_year: Quinn, Sattler, and Weymouth (2023), International Organization 77(4)
refs.why: The reverse channel - currency moves shaping election outcomes - useful for RQ1's identification of elections-vs-markets causality.

refs.title: Tapering talk: The impact of expectations of reduced Federal Reserve security purchases on emerging markets
refs.authors_year: Eichengreen and Gupta (2015), Emerging Markets Review 25
refs.why: Template for which countries suffer outflows in a US policy-shock repricing event - methodology transfers to RQ1's country-level rotation and RQ6's tariff-shock events.

seeds.citation: de Boer, J., Eichler, S., & Rövekamp, I. (2024). Protectionism, Bilateral Integration, and the Cross Section of Exchange Rate Returns in US Presidential Debates. Journal of International Money and Finance, 146, 103134. (Published version: sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0261560624001219; open working-paper version read: CEPIE WP 03/22, EconStor handle 10419/266451)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Using 23 US presidential TV debates (1996-2016) as real-time shocks to election odds, the paper finds currency markets reprice within the 90-minute debate window: when the more protectionist candidate's chances of winning rise, the currencies of countries that trade most heavily with the US (e.g., Mexico, Canada, Singapore) fall against the dollar. Markets price expected tariffs immediately, and the damage concentrates in America's closest trading partners.
seeds.fit: Serves RQ6 directly (tariff/trade-policy expectations as instant repricing events, with country-level winners/losers by candidate) and supports RQ1's country-rotation angle (e.g., why China/Mexico-exposed assets sell off when a protectionist wins).

refs.title: The presidential puzzle: Political cycles and the stock market (Journal of Finance, 58, 1841-1872)
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov (2003)
refs.why: The canonical 'presidential puzzle' paper (higher equity returns under Democrats) - the exact anchor for RQ2.

refs.title: Political cycles and stock returns (Journal of Political Economy, 128, 4011-4045)
refs.authors_year: Pastor & Veronesi (2020)
refs.why: Leading risk-aversion-based explanation of the presidential puzzle - the theory side of RQ2's by-administration return gap.

refs.title: Asset prices, midterm elections, and political uncertainty (Journal of Financial Economics, 141, 276-296)
refs.authors_year: Chan & Marsh (2021)
refs.why: Directly on RQ5: documents and explains the post-midterm equity pattern via resolution of political uncertainty.

refs.title: Company stock price reactions to the 2016 election shock: Trump, taxes, and trade (Journal of Financial Economics, 130, 428-451)
refs.authors_year: Wagner, Zeckhauser & Ziegler (2018)
refs.why: Cross-sectional winners/losers from a surprise election by tax and trade exposure - RQ6's sector-winners-by-party question.

refs.title: The US-Chinese trade war: An event study of stock-market responses (Economic Policy, 35, 519-559)
refs.authors_year: Egger & Zhu (2020)
refs.why: Event-study evidence that tariff announcements are repricing events for exposed stocks - core RQ6 material with a China angle relevant to RQ1.

refs.title: Global political uncertainty and asset prices (Review of Financial Studies, 33, 1737-1780)
refs.authors_year: Brogaard, Dai, Ngo & Zhang (2019)
refs.why: Shows US election uncertainty depresses asset prices globally before resolution - the de-risk-before / re-risk-after mechanism in RQ1.

seeds.citation: Brune, A., Hens, T., Rieger, M. O., & Wang, M. (2015). The war puzzle: Contradictory effects of international conflicts on stock markets. International Review of Economics, 62(1), 1-21. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12232-014-0215-7
seeds.conclusion_plain: Studying major international conflicts since World War II with news-based measures of how likely escalation looked, the authors find that stock markets fall while the chance of war is rising, but tend to rise once war actually breaks out - unless the outbreak comes as a surprise, in which case prices drop. They show this "war puzzle" cannot be explained by standard stories like investor risk aversion or expectations that the war will be over quickly.
seeds.fit: This is the anchor paper for RQ4: it defines the war puzzle (buildup-down / outbreak-up) that the war-and-markets analysis tests.

refs.title: The effects of war risk on US financial markets
refs.authors_year: Rigobon & Sack (2005), Journal of Banking & Finance 29(7): 1769-1789
refs.why: Quantifies how rising war risk (Iraq 2003) was priced into equities, bonds, oil and the dollar before the outbreak - the buildup-down half of RQ4.

refs.title: War and the world economy: stock market reactions to international conflicts
refs.authors_year: Schneider & Troeger (2006), Journal of Conflict Resolution 50(5): 623-645
refs.why: Event-study evidence on how global stock markets react to international conflicts across several war episodes - core RQ4 evidence.

refs.title: The economic effects of violent conflict: evidence from asset market reactions
refs.authors_year: Guidolin & La Ferrara (2010), Journal of Peace Research 47(6): 671-684
refs.why: Broad cross-conflict study finding asset markets sometimes react positively to conflict onset - directly supports the outbreak-up side of the war puzzle (RQ4).

refs.title: History as reflected in capital markets: the case of World War II
refs.authors_year: Frey & Kucher (2000), Journal of Economic History 60(2): 468-496
refs.why: Shows WWII war news was priced into government bond markets - historical baseline for war-and-markets repricing in RQ4.

refs.title: Political news and stock prices: the case of Saddam Hussein contracts
refs.authors_year: Amihud & Wohl (2004), Journal of Banking & Finance 28(5): 1185-1200
refs.why: Links a market-traded probability of a political/war outcome to stock and oil prices - method template for event-probability repricing used in RQ4 (and transferable to RQ1/RQ6).

refs.title: Using markets to inform policy: the case of the Iraq War
refs.authors_year: Wolfers & Zitzewitz (2009), Economica 76(302): 225-250
refs.why: Uses prediction-market war probabilities to estimate the expected effect of war on equities and oil - complements the buildup-vs-outbreak decomposition in RQ4.

seeds.citation: Cortes, Gustavo S., Angela Vossmeyer, and Marc D. Weidenmier (2022). "Stock Volatility and the War Puzzle." NBER Working Paper No. 29837, March 2022 (revised May 2024 as "Stock Volatility and the War Puzzle: The Military Demand Channel").
seeds.conclusion_plain: US stock market volatility is roughly a third lower during wars and conflicts, and the paper explains why: massive, government-guaranteed defense spending makes company profits much easier to predict, which calms markets. Using 100+ years of defense-spending data, they show military outlays reduce volatility at the aggregate, sector, and even global level, and analyst earnings forecasts for defense firms became visibly tighter after 9/11 and the Afghanistan/Iraq invasions.
seeds.fit: Directly serves RQ4: it is the canonical resolution of the war puzzle (volatility falls once conflict starts) via the defense-spending/military-demand channel, including defense-sector evidence.

refs.title: Why does stock market volatility change over time?
refs.authors_year: Schwert, G. W. (1989), Journal of Finance
refs.why: Origin of the war puzzle itself — two centuries of US volatility data showing wartime volatility is anomalously low (RQ4 cornerstone).

refs.title: The boats that did not sail: Asset price volatility in a natural experiment
refs.authors_year: Koudijs, P. (2016), Journal of Finance
refs.why: Natural experiment on how war-related news arrival drives stock volatility — clean identification for RQ4 war-news/market dynamics.

refs.title: Rare disasters and asset markets in the twentieth century
refs.authors_year: Barro, R. J. (2006), Quarterly Journal of Economics
refs.why: Treats wars as the key rare disasters pricing equities — theory backbone for the buildup-down/outbreak-up pattern in RQ4.

refs.title: Can time-varying risk of rare disasters explain aggregate stock market volatility?
refs.authors_year: Wachter, J. A. (2013), Journal of Finance
refs.why: Time-varying disaster (war) risk as the driver of volatility swings — the model RQ4's pre-war fear vs wartime calm maps onto.

refs.title: Measuring economic policy uncertainty
refs.authors_year: Baker, S. R., N. Bloom, and S. J. Davis (2016), Quarterly Journal of Economics
refs.why: EPU index spikes around elections and trade-policy fights — workhorse measure for RQ1 (election de-risking) and RQ6 (tariff shocks).

refs.title: Debt and Taxes in Eight U.S. Wars and Two Insurrections
refs.authors_year: Hall, G. and T. J. Sargent (2020), NBER WP 27115
refs.why: How the US financed its wars (debt vs taxes) — fiscal channel linking wartime spending to asset markets for RQ4.

seeds.citation: Leigh, A., Wolfers, J., & Zitzewitz, E. (2003). "What Do Financial Markets Think of War in Iraq?" NBER Working Paper No. 9587.
seeds.conclusion_plain: Using prices of a betting-market contract on Saddam Hussein being ousted ("Saddam Securities"), the paper finds that markets expected war with Iraq to raise oil prices by about $10/barrel and lower U.S. stocks by around 15%, with a meaningful chance of much worse outcomes. The equity decline works mainly through lower expected future company earnings (weakest sectors: consumer discretionary and airlines; gold and oil firms gain), not through investors demanding extra compensation for risk.
seeds.fit: Core RQ4 paper: shows how markets price war risk before outbreak (buildup-down leg of the war puzzle), with sector winners/losers and cross-country effects that also feed RQ1 and RQ3 (gold as war hedge).

refs.title: History as Reflected in Capital Markets: The Case of World War II
refs.authors_year: Frey & Kucher (2000), Journal of Economic History 60(2)
refs.why: Direct RQ4 ancestor: uses wartime asset prices to read how markets valued WWII events, the historical foundation of the war-and-markets literature.

refs.title: How Government Bond Prices Reflect Wartime Events: The Case of the Stockholm Market
refs.authors_year: Waldenström & Frey (2002), U. Zurich IEW Working Paper No. 102
refs.why: RQ4: bond-market repricing around wartime news from a neutral country, a clean event-study template for war shocks.

refs.title: Results from a Dozen Years of Election Futures Markets Research
refs.authors_year: Berg, Forsythe, Nelson & Rietz (2001), in Handbook of Experimental Economics Results
refs.why: RQ1/RQ2/RQ5: establishes election prediction markets as the probability instrument for election event studies, the same identification trick our election work needs.

refs.title: What Moves Stock Prices?
refs.authors_year: Cutler, Poterba & Summers (1989), Journal of Portfolio Management 15(3)
refs.why: RQ4/RQ6 framing: canonical evidence on how much political and news events actually move aggregate stock prices, baseline for treating policy shocks as repricing events.

refs.title: The Economic Consequences of a War with Iraq
refs.authors_year: Nordhaus (2002), in War with Iraq: Costs, Consequences, and Alternatives (Am. Academy of Arts & Sciences)
refs.why: RQ4: the standard macro cost-of-war estimate that the market-based estimates are benchmarked against (markets priced war as costlier than analysts did).

seeds.citation: Caldara, Dario, and Matteo Iacoviello. 2022. "Measuring Geopolitical Risk." American Economic Review 112 (4): 1194-1225. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.20191823
seeds.conclusion_plain: The authors build a newspaper-based index of geopolitical risk going back to 1900 and show that when geopolitical risk rises, investment and employment fall and the odds of an economic disaster go up — and the damage comes both from the threat of bad events and from the events actually happening. Firms and industries more exposed to geopolitics cut investment the most.
seeds.fit: Backbone measurement paper for RQ3 (is gold's rise geopolitical?) and RQ4 (its threat-vs-realization split maps directly onto the buildup-down/outbreak-up war puzzle); the GPR index is the natural conditioning variable for both modules.

refs.title: Time-Varying Rare Disaster Risk and Stock Returns
refs.authors_year: Berkman, Jacobsen & Lee (2011), Journal of Financial Economics
refs.why: Uses international political crises to show stocks fall when crises start and recover when they resolve — the closest academic anchor for our RQ4 war puzzle.

refs.title: Collateral Damage: Trade Disruption and the Economic Impact of War
refs.authors_year: Glick & Taylor (2010), Review of Economics and Statistics
refs.why: Quantifies how wars destroy trade flows — bridges RQ4 (wars and markets) and RQ6 (trade-disruption repricing).

refs.title: Measuring Economic Policy Uncertainty
refs.authors_year: Baker, Bloom & Davis (2016), Quarterly Journal of Economics
refs.why: The EPU index spikes around elections and trade-policy fights — the standard uncertainty control for RQ1 election de-risking and RQ6 tariff shocks.

refs.title: Firm-Level Political Risk: Measurement and Effects
refs.authors_year: Hassan, Hollander, van Lent & Tahoun (2019), Quarterly Journal of Economics
refs.why: Measures which firms are exposed to political risk (incl. trade/national security) — useful for RQ6 sector winners by party and RQ2 administration regimes.

refs.title: Identifying Government Spending Shocks: It's All in the Timing
refs.authors_year: Ramey (2011), Quarterly Journal of Economics
refs.why: Military spending 'news' shocks dated from narrative records — directly relevant to RQ4 defense-sector buildup dynamics.

seeds.citation: Berkman, H., Jacobsen, B., & Lee, J. B. (2011). Time-varying rare disaster risk and stock returns. Journal of Financial Economics, 101(2), 313-332.
seeds.conclusion_plain: Using 447 international political crises from 1918-2006, the authors show that when the perceived chance of a major crisis or war rises, world stock prices fall and markets become more volatile, and when crises resolve, stocks recover. Industries more exposed to this crisis risk earn higher average returns, meaning investors are paid extra to bear political-disaster risk.
seeds.fit: Core evidence for RQ4 (war puzzle: crisis buildup depresses stocks, resolution/outbreak clarity lifts them; geopolitical risk is priced), with side relevance to RQ3 (geopolitical risk as a driver of safe-haven demand).

refs.title: The effects of war risk on US financial markets
refs.authors_year: Rigobon & Sack (2005)
refs.why: Directly measures how rising war probability (Iraq 2003) hit US equities, yields, oil and the dollar - the buildup-down leg of our RQ4 war puzzle.

refs.title: Political news and stock prices: The case of Saddam Hussein contracts
refs.authors_year: Amihud & Wohl (2004)
refs.why: Uses prediction-market war odds to show stocks repricing as war/outcome probabilities shift - clean event evidence for RQ4 outbreak dynamics.

refs.title: World War II as reflected on capital markets
refs.authors_year: Frey & Kucher (2000)
refs.why: Asset prices around WWII turning points - historical anchor for the RQ4 war-and-markets pattern.

refs.title: Using Markets to Inform Policy: The Case of the Iraq War
refs.authors_year: Wolfers & Zitzewitz (2009)
refs.why: Estimates the equity and oil-market cost priced into Iraq-war probability - quantifies the pre-war de-risking channel in RQ4.

refs.title: How Government Bond Prices Reflect Wartime Events
refs.authors_year: Waldenström & Frey (2002)
refs.why: Shows wartime events priced into sovereign bonds - cross-asset confirmation of war repricing for RQ4.

refs.title: War Politics: An Economic, Rational-Voter Framework
refs.authors_year: Hess & Orphanides (1995)
refs.why: Models incumbents' electoral incentives to start conflicts - bridges elections (RQ1/RQ2) with war outbreaks (RQ4).

seeds.citation: Boungou, W., & Yatie, A. (2022). The impact of the Ukraine-Russia war on world stock market returns. Economics Letters, 215, 110516. (Open-access working-paper PDF read: Bordeaux Economics WP 2022-06, http://bordeauxeconomicswp.u-bordeaux.fr/2022/2022-06.pdf)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Using daily stock returns for 94 countries from 22 Jan to 24 Mar 2022, the paper finds the Ukraine-Russia war pushed stock markets down worldwide, with the biggest drops in the first two weeks after the 24 Feb invasion and a fading effect afterward. Losses were largest for countries bordering Ukraine/Russia and for countries that condemned the invasion at the UN (condemning countries fell about 3-4x more than neutral ones).
seeds.fit: RQ4 (wars and markets): the canonical outbreak-day event study - global equity drawdown at invasion onset that decays, with cross-country heterogeneity by proximity and political alignment - the 'outbreak' half of the war puzzle baseline.

refs.title: War and stock markets: The effect of World War Two on the British stock market
refs.authors_year: Hudson & Urquhart (2015), International Review of Financial Analysis 40, 166-177
refs.why: Core RQ4 war-puzzle reference: long-horizon study of how war events (good/bad news, buildup vs outbreak) moved an equity market through WWII.

refs.title: Wars and markets: How bond values reflect the Second World War
refs.authors_year: Frey & Kucher (2001; printed as 2003 in the paper's bibliography), Economica 68(271), 317-333
refs.why: Classic war-pricing study - market prices (government bonds) as real-time barometers of WWII war news; foundation for RQ4 buildup/outbreak repricing.

refs.title: Armageddon and the stock market: US, Canadian and Mexican market responses to the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis
refs.authors_year: Burdekin & Siklos (2022), Quarterly Review of Economics and Finance 84, 112-127
refs.why: Geopolitical near-war crisis event study across three markets - directly tests the threat-buildup-down / resolution-up dynamic in RQ4.

refs.title: Naval disasters, World War Two and the British stock market
refs.authors_year: Hudson & Urquhart (2022), Research in International Business and Finance 59, 101556
refs.why: Granular war-news event study (battle outcomes -> equity reaction); useful for RQ4's claim that markets reprice on war developments, not just outbreak.

refs.title: The price of terror: The effects of terrorism on stock market returns and volatility
refs.authors_year: Arin, Ciferri & Spagnolo (2008), Economics Letters 101(3), 164-167
refs.why: Geopolitical-shock event study on both returns and volatility - methodological and substantive bridge for RQ4 (conflict shocks) and the volatility side of RQ2/RQ3 regimes.

seeds.citation: Amiti, M., M. Gomez, S. H. Kong, and D. E. Weinstein (2026). "Trade Protection, Stock Market Returns, and Welfare." Working paper, May 20, 2026 version (supersedes "The Effect of the U.S.-China Trade War on U.S. Investment").
seeds.conclusion_plain: Tariff announcements in the 2018-19 US-China trade war caused large, lasting stock-market drops (cumulative -11.5% US, -20.6% China), with China-exposed firms hit hardest, and those announcement-day losses correctly predicted later declines in real profits, sales, jobs, and investment. Reading those market reactions through their model, the authors conclude the trade war made Americans substantially worse off — far more than standard static tariff models suggest — and the 2025-26 tariff episode shows nearly identical market patterns (-11.4% cumulative on tariff-increase days).
seeds.fit: Core paper for RQ6: it treats tariff announcements as repricing events, identifies sector/firm winners and losers by China exposure, and shows announcement-day returns forecast real outcomes.

refs.title: The Return to Protectionism
refs.authors_year: Fajgelbaum, Goldberg, Kennedy & Khandelwal (2020), QJE
refs.why: Canonical estimate of who won and lost from the 2018 tariff waves — the real-economy benchmark for RQ6 tariff-shock repricing.

refs.title: Trade Networks and Firm Value: Evidence from the U.S.-China Trade War
refs.authors_year: Huang, Lin, Liu & Tang (2023), JIE
refs.why: Firm-level stock reactions to trade-war news by supply-chain exposure — directly the RQ6 winners/losers-by-exposure design.

refs.title: Using Equity Market Reactions to Infer Exposure to Trade Liberalization
refs.authors_year: Greenland, Ion, Lopresti & Schott (2024), JIE forthcoming
refs.why: Methodology for extracting trade-policy exposure from stock returns — reusable template for our RQ6 event studies.

refs.title: Nationalism and Economic Exchange: Evidence from Shocks to Sino-Japanese Relations
refs.authors_year: Fisman, Hamao & Wang (2014), RFS
refs.why: Geopolitical-tension shocks moving exposed firms' stock prices — bridges RQ4 (conflict and markets) and RQ3 (political risk pricing).

refs.title: The Economic Effects of Brexit: Evidence from the Stock Market
refs.authors_year: Breinlich, Leromain, Novy, Sampson & Usman (2018), Fiscal Studies
refs.why: A surprise vote as a market repricing event with country/sector rotation — closest analogue to our RQ1/RQ6 election-shock designs.

refs.title: Measuring Economic Policy Uncertainty
refs.authors_year: Baker, Bloom & Davis (2016), QJE
refs.why: The EPU index that spikes around elections and trade fights — standard conditioning variable for RQ1/RQ2 election-uncertainty work.

seeds.citation: Al-Kharusi, S. and Elroukh, A.W. (2026), "Trump Liberation Day tariffs and stock market reactions: new insights from global analysis", Studies in Economics and Finance, Vol. 43 No. 2, pp. 415-435. doi: 10.1108/SEF-08-2025-0536
seeds.conclusion_plain: After the April 2, 2025 "Liberation Day" tariff announcement, stock markets in most of the 77 countries studied fell sharply and in sync (losses beyond -15% in some countries, over -10% in the US itself), but countries hit with higher tariff rates did not fall more than those facing the baseline 10%. What determined how hard a market fell was not the tariff rate it was assigned but how tied that country's trade and financial system is to the US.
seeds.fit: RQ6 - direct global event-study evidence that tariff/trade-policy announcements act as synchronized market repricing events, with transmission via trade exposure and US financial integration.

refs.title: U.S. tariffs and stock prices
refs.authors_year: Yilmazkuday (2025), Finance Research Letters
refs.why: Core RQ6 evidence: how US tariff announcements move equity prices.

refs.title: Financial market reactions to U.S. tariff announcements: Evidence from trade-surplus and trade-deficit countries
refs.authors_year: Rao, Lucey & Kumar (2025), Finance Research Letters
refs.why: RQ6 with a country-rotation angle (surplus vs deficit countries) that also feeds RQ1's cross-country flow story.

refs.title: Share price reactions to tariff imposition announcements during the first Trump administration
refs.authors_year: Wengerek, Uhde & Hippert (2025), Finance Research Letters
refs.why: RQ6 event-study of first-term Trump tariffs - a clean prior-episode comparison for Liberation Day.

refs.title: Tariff exposure and sectoral vulnerability: Evidence from equity market responses to the 2025 U.S. trade shock
refs.authors_year: Kaczmarek, Demir, Rouatbi & Zaremba (2025), Research in International Business and Finance
refs.why: RQ6 sector winners/losers: which industries reprice most on the 2025 tariff shock.

refs.title: The effects of the Russia-Ukraine war and the Wagner Group coup on defense stocks in Europe: an event study analysis
refs.authors_year: Covachev & Fazakas (2024), Studies in Economics and Finance
refs.why: RQ4 directly: defense-sector returns around war outbreak and escalation events.

refs.title: Searching for safe haven assets against American and European stocks during the Russo-Ukrainian War
refs.authors_year: Belhassine & Riahi (2024), Studies in Economics and Finance
refs.why: RQ3/RQ4: tests gold and other safe havens during a geopolitical war shock.

seeds.citation: Wang, Xiaoli, and Claire Guo. 2025. Presidential Partisanship and Sectoral ETF Performance in U.S. Equity Markets. Risks 13(10): 201. https://doi.org/10.3390/risks13100201 (DOI verified resolving to the MDPI page; MDPI blocks bot fetches, so abstract and references were confirmed via Semantic Scholar and Crossref records for this DOI)
seeds.conclusion_plain: Using GICS sector ETFs, the paper finds that U.S. stock sectors show different return and volatility patterns under Republican versus Democratic presidencies, so the party in the White House matters at the sector level, not just for the overall market. But the sector winners are not always the intuitive ones (e.g., energy underperformed during the pro-fossil-fuel first Trump term), so party affiliation alone is an unreliable sector-picking rule.
seeds.fit: Core for RQ2 (returns/volatility regimes by administration) and RQ6 (sector winners by winning party) — it is the sector-level bridge between the presidential puzzle and partisan sector rotation.

refs.title: The presidential puzzle: Political cycles and the stock market (Journal of Finance)
refs.authors_year: Santa-Clara & Valkanov, 2003
refs.why: The canonical RQ2 paper — documents and names the Democrat return premium across administrations.

refs.title: Political cycles and stock returns (Journal of Political Economy)
refs.authors_year: Pastor & Veronesi, 2020
refs.why: Leading theoretical resolution of the presidential puzzle (risk aversion drives both who wins and subsequent returns) — RQ2.

refs.title: Impact of U.S. presidential elections on stock markets' volatility: Does incumbent president's party matter? (Finance Research Letters)
refs.authors_year: Mnasri & Essaddam, 2021
refs.why: Election-window volatility conditioned on the incumbent's party — serves RQ1 de-risk/re-risk timing and RQ2 volatility regimes.

refs.title: Does the U.S. president affect the stock market? (Journal of Financial Markets)
refs.authors_year: Montone, 2022
refs.why: Modern re-examination of partisan return differences and their channel — direct RQ2 evidence.

refs.title: Government partisanship, elections, and the stock market: Examining American and British stock returns, 1930-2000 (American Journal of Political Science)
refs.authors_year: Leblang & Mukherjee, 2005
refs.why: Long-sample partisan returns including election periods, with a UK cross-check — RQ2 plus RQ1 election-window behavior.

refs.title: Political orientation of government and stock market returns (Applied Financial Economics Letters)
refs.authors_year: Bialkowski, Gottschalk & Wisniewski, 2007
refs.why: Tests whether the left/right return gap generalizes internationally — robustness check on the RQ2 presidential puzzle.

seeds.citation: Baker, S. R., Bloom, N., & Davis, S. J. (2016). Measuring Economic Policy Uncertainty. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 131(4), 1593-1636. (Loaded version: NBER Working Paper 21633, October 2015.)
